text: The robotic arms of the da Vinci SI system are used to mobilize and dissect the angle of His in a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass procedure.

The surgeon opens the pars flaccida using blunt dissection, exposing the lesser curvature of the stomach. This provides access for posterior dissection, with occasional use of electrocautery to address any visible adhesions.

The surgeon places an Endo GIA 60 vascular stapler to divide the mesentery along the lesser curvature, taking care to avoid stapling the gastric body. Further dissection is performed to create a window to the angle of His.
The surgeon uses an EndoGIA-XX green load Echelon stapler to divide the stomach transversely after creating a posterior window behind the stomach.
The surgeon uses a sixty green load stapler to vertically dissect and ligate the gastric pouch, completing the transaction with an additional staple fire.

The completed gastric pouch is visible as the surgeon elevates the omentum and transverse colon to identify the ligament of Treitz. Adhesions between the omentum and lower abdomen are divided using a ligature device.

The surgeon uses a ligature to split the omentum, reducing tension on the roux limbs. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the bowel is measured and brought up to the gastric pouch, with adjustments made to the length if tension is observed.

The surgeon uses the third arm to stabilize the roux limb while switching to needle drivers in the right and left hands. A 13-inch dyed, 2-0 Vicryl suture is used for the gastro-digital anastomosis, running continuously for the posterior row and then serving as the anterior row. The surgeon adjusts the liver retractor, accidentally puncturing the left lobe of the liver, which is tamponaded with the retractor.
The surgeon ties a 13-inch, 2-0 varical suture, leaving a tail to secure the anterior row for final stitching. The suture is then run continuously along the posterior row of the staple line.
The surgeon exchanges a needle driver for Bentham bob scissors and coordinates with the anesthesiologist to place a bougie into the gastric pouch.

The surgeon locates the bougie within the gastric pouch and uses electrocautery to create a gastrotomy approximately 1.5 centimeters in size, sufficient for the bougie to pass through. Subsequently, an enterotomy is made in the small bowel, initially kept small due to its tendency to dilate easily.

The surgeon switches back to the needle driver and introduces an undyed, double-ended suture into the abdomen. The suture consists of two six-inch sutures tied together, intended for the inner row of anastomosis. The assistant struggles to tie the suture ends securely, causing a brief pause in the procedure.

The surgeon begins suturing with a double-ended, two-oh undyed vibral suture, starting at the posterior corner. The suture is run posteriorly, with the other end brought around anteriorly, ensuring the tails are kept short to avoid confusion.

The surgeon begins suturing the inner row anteriorly, carefully inserting the bougie through the anastomosis into the small bowel to prevent backwalling of the sutures during the anterior row stitching.

The surgeon brings the initial suture around the anterior aspect of the anastomosis, forming the outer layer. The suture is extended across the anterior until it reaches the initial tail, where it is secured.
The surgeon uses an EndoGIA-A-Sixty stapler to divide the small bowel close to the completed anastomosis, leaving a half to one centimeter apparent limb without a candy cane on the bypass.

The surgeon measures the roux limb, selecting a length between 130 and 150 centimeters for patients with diabetes. The two ends of the small bowel are sutured together using a 2-0 silk suture. Mobile enterotomies are created with a ligature, followed by a stapled side-to-side anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the common enterotomy using a running suture technique with a 2-0 Vicryl suture.

The surgeon closes the mesentery defect using a silk suture, starting at the crotch of the defect. The laparotide plate is visible at the end of the defect, which measures approximately seven inches in length. The closure is performed in a running fashion to ensure no space is left, preventing future complications.

The laparotri is positioned to secure the surgical site. Remaining needles are removed from the abdomen, marking the completion of the procedure. An upper endoscopy is performed, and a leak test is conducted to verify the integrity of the anastomosis. Robotic arms are used to occlude the roux limb, facilitating intubation of the gastric pouch for an accurate leak test.
